task: The user wants help deciding whether to extend Aiki with a browser-based interface launched by `aiki serve`, and which standout features would make it a compelling hackathon project. AND ALSO: The user wants to decide whether building a local web server and UI for their existing AI orchestration CLI tool will improve their chances at a hackathon, and what standout features to include.
constraints: Aiki currently orchestrates locally installed Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs.
The published package is local-first and does not require API keys.
The proposed interface should be launched from the installed CLI using `aiki serve` and accessed through a browser on a local port.
The result is intended for a NamasteDev hackathon showcase.
Must be completed within the hackathon timeframe
Must operate locally leveraging existing installed CLIs without requiring API keys
Must be an extension of the existing aiki-cli tool
unknowns: Hackathon rules, judging criteria, submission format, and restrictions.
Hackathon deadline and available implementation time.
Evidence comparing Aiki's synthesized output with individual model outputs.
Provider CLI behavior under concurrent requests, cancellation, failures, and long-running sessions.
Security boundaries for exposing local CLI processes through an HTTP server.
The primary audience and demonstration use case.
The judging criteria and theme of the NamasteDev hackathon (site blocked)
Technical details on how Aiki currently spawns and manages the underlying CLI processes (e.g., handling interactive prompts)
The remaining time available for the hackathon
success_criteria: Reach a clear build-or-do-not-build verdict for the web interface.
Define an improved end-to-end user workflow.
Identify differentiated, high-impact features suitable for the hackathon.
Separate essential demo scope from ambitious extensions.
Address feasibility, reliability, and local execution risks.
Functional local server bridging the CLI tools to a web frontend
Real-time visual representation of multi-LLM research and output
Implementation of 'ultra-level' features that differentiate it from standard chat wrappers

domain_dimensions.label: Model-council legibility
domain_dimensions.rationale: The interface must make disagreement, critique, evidence, and synthesis understandable enough that judges can see value beyond parallel chat responses.

domain_dimensions.label: Process Orchestration Feasibility
domain_dimensions.rationale: Wrapping interactive CLIs in a web server often requires complex pseudoterminal (pty) management and streaming (WebSockets) to maintain the 'live' feel, which can be brittle.

domain_dimensions.label: Local CLI execution boundary
domain_dimensions.rationale: Launching privileged local processes from browser requests introduces security, permission, isolation, cancellation, and lifecycle concerns that may determine feasibility.

domain_dimensions.label: Hackathon Meta-Strategy
domain_dimensions.rationale: Hackathons heavily weight visual appeal and live demoability; understanding if a dev-tool UI aligns with the specific hackathon's undisclosed theme is critical for ROI.

domain_dimensions.label: Synthesis provenance
domain_dimensions.rationale: Showing which models contributed claims, critiques, and evidence can determine whether the final answer appears trustworthy rather than opaque.

alternatives: Local web control plane via `aiki serve`
Hybrid CLI with optional visual companion
Build the `aiki serve` web UI with advanced real-time orchestration features
Keep the project CLI-only but add advanced terminal UI (TUI) features
Pivot to a different project for the hackathon
success_bar: A successful decision yields a hackathon-feasible experience that visibly demonstrates why multi-model orchestration produces more trustworthy or useful results than a conventional AI chat interface.
evidence_supplied: The fetched npm registry snapshot identifies aiki-cli version 0.3.1.
The package describes Aiki as a local-first AI model council for code review and idea stress-testing.
The package currently uses Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs without API keys.
The hackathon page was supplied but could not be fetched because automated access returned HTTP 403.
Aiki is an existing npm package (aiki-cli v0.3.1)
Aiki orchestrates Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs locally without API keys
The target hackathon is at namastedev.com/hackathon

Hackathon deadline and available implementation time.
Evidence comparing Aiki's synthesized output with individual model outputs.
